S**tate which storage system and dataset you have chosen.** 

We have chosen to use MongoDB as our storage system and the AirBnB dataset as the data that we will be working with.

**(2 points)** Show how the example data can be represented in the data model of your data store. Mention any indexes you will create.

We are planning to represent the data in 4 MongoDB collections, each mapping to one of the provided CSVs as follows

**listings**

This is our main collection \- we have most of the details about the listing that are more static and form the basic representation of the listing. Host details are embedded here as well. 

```json
{
  "_id": "12345",
  "city": "Portland",
  "name": "Cozy Studio",
  "neighborhood": "Downtown",
  "room_type": "Entire home/apt",
  "property_type": "Apartment",
  "accommodates": 4,
  "amenities": ["Wifi", "Kitchen", "Parking"],
  "price": 85.00,
  "rating": 4.7,
  "min_nights": 2,
  "max_nights": 30,
  "listing_url": "https://...",
  "description": "...",
  "host": {
    "host_id": "99",
    "host_name": "Alice"
  }
}
```

**calendar**

```json
{
  "_id": ObjectId("..."),
  "listing_id": "12345",
  "date": ISODate("2024-03-15"),
  "available": true,
  "price": 90.00,
  "minimum_nights": 2,
  "maximum_nights": 14
}
```

**reviews**

```json
{
  "_id": ObjectId("..."),
  "listing_id": "12345",
  "reviewer_id": "456",
  "reviewer_name": "Bob",
  "date": ISODate("2024-12-01"),
  "comments": "Great place!"
}
```

**neighborhoods**

```json
{
  "_id": ObjectId("..."),
  "city": "Portland",
  "neighborhood": "Pearl District"
}
```

**Indexes we are planning to have based on what query they would be useful for**

| Collection | Index | Query |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **listings** | **\_id (auto)** | **Default primary key** |
| **listings** | **{ city: 1, neighborhood: 1 }** | **Filter by city \+ group/filter by neighborhood (Q1, Q2)** |
| **listings** | **{ city: 1, room\_type: 1 }** | **Filter Entire home/apt in a city (Q3, Q4)** |
| **listings** | **{ host.host\_id: 1, city: 1 }** | **Find other listings by same host in same city (Q6)** |
| **calendar** | **{ listing\_id: 1, date: 1, available: 1 }** | **Availability lookups by listing and date (Q1, Q3, Q4, Q6)** |
| **calendar** | **{ date: 1, available: 1 }** | **Scan availability across all listings for a given month (Q2)** |
| **reviews** | **{ date: 1, listing\_id: 1 }** | **Filter reviews by month, carry listing\_id (Q5)** |
| **reviews** | **{ reviewer\_id: 1, listing\_id: 1, date: 1 }** | **Find reviewers who reviewed the same listing multiple times (Q6)** |
| **neighborhoods** | **{ city: 1, neighborhood: 1 }** | **Lookup neighborhoods by city (Q2)** |

**(2 points)** Describe at least one variant representation you considered during the design phase.

We considered having a fully embedded representation where the reviews and the calendar information was embedded within the listings document. This would have looked like a nested array within the listing with ids of calendar and reviews that would have then contained dictionaries containing the data from those CSV files. This would have looked something like 

```json
{
  "_id": "12345",
  "name": "Cozy Studio",
  "calendar": [
    { "date": "2024-03-01", "available": true, "minimum_nights": 2 },
    ...365+ entries
  ],
  "reviews": [
    { "reviewer_id": "456", "date": "2024-12-01", "comments": "Great!" },
    ...
  ]
}
```

**(2 points)** Explain why you believe your final design is the preferred one.

Our design has several advantages over the fully nested structure. First, full-nesting causes major limitations on indexing possibilities. Since a lot of the queries we have to execute require us to index into specific keys from within reviews and calendars, creating indexes for embedded arrays would’ve been a significantly more cumbersome and less efficient process.

Secondly, not all queries center the listing. For example, queries 4 and 5 require us to scan calendar and review databases across various listings which would’ve been excessively inefficient in a nested structure where the listing was the primary access point to the underlying data. 

Finally, considering the size of the dataset, it is likely that we would have exceeded MongoDBs 16MB document cap \- especially if a listing were to contain many reviews and have availability information on a wide range of dates. 

**(4 points)** Describe the process you will use to restructure the data to fit your model—including any cleaning steps you think will be needed—and how you plan to load it. (Note, you need not load portions of data items into your system if you determine they won’t be required for the queries.)

We will use the ETL process to restructure and load the data for our model. We will first extract and decompress the “.[csv.gz](http://csv.gz)” files from the Inside Airbnb website. We will then transform the data into a dataframe using Pandas in Python. This is to make it easier only keeping fields we want and to prune out unnecessary fields to keep the data size smaller. For the listings dataset there are many fields. We will only retain fields that are relevant to our queries. So we are keeping fields such as id, name, property\_type, accommodates, price, minimum\_nights, maximum\_nights, host\_name, host\_id, description, neighbourhood\_cleansed, listing\_url, room\_type, review\_scores\_rating, and amenities. We will drop all other fields such as host\_picture\_url. We will then rename some of the fields to match the JSON format. So we will rename id to id, minimum\_nights and maximum\_nights to min\_nights and max\_nights, neighbourhood\_cleansed to neighborhood, and review\_scores\_rating to rating. We will embed host\_id and host\_name into the host object. We will also convert all fields requiring numerical values into numerical values from strings and also parse through the amenities field to create an array. For all the entries we will also attach a field storing the city from which we downloaded the data from. For the calendar dataset we will take the listing\_id, date, available, price, minimum\_nights, and maximum\_nights fields and drop the rest. We will need to convert price, minimum and maximum nights into numerical data. We will also parse date into ISODate, and convert ‘t’ to true and ‘f’ to false for available. For the reviews dataset we will keep listing\_id, reviewer\_id, reviewer\_name, date, and comments. We will convert date to ISODate, and drop all other fields not required by the JSON. For the neighborhoods dataset we will standardize city and neighborhood names and remove duplicates.

To ensure consistency across collections, we will enforce that listing\_id in both calendar and reviews datasets matches the \_id in the listings dataset. We will also remove all records with missing critical fields. Due to the large dataset size, we will load the cleaned data into MongoDB using batch inserts or mongoimport and create indexes after loading to optimize query performance.

**(7 points)** For the six queries in the appropriate dataset description document, give implementation strategies in pseudo-code based on the capabilities of your data store and the indexes you plan to define. Be sure there are enough comments to explain the strategy of each. 

**Query 1: AirBnB Search**

**Indexes Used:** listings { city, neighborhood }, calendar { listing\_id, date, available }

**High-level logical steps / Pseudocode** 

1. Search calendar collection for entries where the date falls in the given two-day period, availability is true, and the minimum\_nights \<= 2 \<= maximum\_nights. (all of this should be easily accomplished efficiently with the index we’ve defined)  
2. Group these results by listing\_id and only keep the listing-ids where both dates are available.  
3. Cross-reference with the listings collection to filter for Portland (city is one of our indices)  
4. For each result, we retrieve the name, neighborhood, room type, accommodations, property types, amenities and nightly price from the listings collection  
5. Finally, sort the results by average rating descending (we will exclude the listings with no ratings provided)

**Query 2: Neighborhoods with No Listings**

**Indexes Used:** calendar { date, available }, listings { city, neighborhood }, neighborhoods { city, neighborhood }

**High-level logical steps / Pseudocode** 

1. Search calendar collection for all listing\_ids with at least one available day in the target month (done with calendar index)  
2. Find the city and neighborhood for each of the listing\_ids retrieved from the previous step (using \_id from listings collection)  
3. Get full set of neighborhoods from the neighborhoods collection  
4. Return any neighborhood that is in the neighborhoods collection but is not in the set of neighborhoods retrieved in step 2

**Query 3: Availability for Booking (Salem)**

**Indexes Used:** listings { city, room\_type }, calendar { listing\_id, date, available }

**High-level logical steps / Pseudocode** 

1. Search listings collection for listings located in Salem with the wanted room\_type (done with listings index)  
2. Cross-search calendar collection to check availability during the requested period  
3. Group results by listing\_id  
4. Return listing details of valid listings

**Query 4: Booking Trend by Month (Portland)**

**Indexes Used:** listings { city, room\_type }, calendar { listing\_id, date, available }

**High-level logical steps / Pseudocode** 

1. Search listings collection for listings located in Portland (done with listing index)  
2. Search calendar collection for listings in the year we want  
3. Identify booked dates in the year  
4. Group bookings by month and return chronologically by month

**Query 5: Reviews by City per December**

**Indexes Used:** reviews { date, listing\_id }, listings \_id (auto) 

**High-level logical steps / Pseudocode** 

1. Search reviews collection to find reviews in December of the wanted year (using reviews index)  
2. Cross-search with listings to find the city for each reviewed listing  
3. Group reviews by city and return by number of reviews

**Query 6: Reminder to Book Again**

**Indexes Used:** reviews { reviewer\_id, listing\_id, date }, calendar { listing\_id, date, available }, listings { host.host\_id, city }

**High-level logical steps / Pseudocode** 

1. Search reviews to find listings that the user has previously reviewed (using reviews index) and remove duplicate ids  
2. Cross-search with calendar collection to see current availability of these listings  
3. Identify listings that have future availability and retrieve listing and host information  
4. Return suggestions to user of these listings as a reminder to book again

**(3 points)** Include a screenshot that shows you have loaded at least a few data items into your system.
